title: Do I Really Have to Take the SAT or ACT Anymore?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You've heard the news: more colleges are going test-optional. Some have dropped testing requirements entirely. Does that mean you can skip the SAT or ACT? The answer is more complicated than yes or no. Here is how to decide based on your specific situation.

Test-Optional Does Not Mean Test-Blind

Test-optional means you can choose whether to submit your scores. The college will review your application either way. Test-blind means the college will not consider scores even if you submit them. Very few schools are test-blind.

If you have strong scores, submit them. They can only help. If you have weak scores, don't submit them. The college will focus on your GPA, essays, and activities. This is the advantage of test-optional policies.

When You Should Take the SAT or ACT

You should take the test if:
• You're a strong test-taker (you score above the median for your target schools)
• You're applying to competitive scholarships that require scores
• You're applying to engineering or business programs that still value scores
• You're homeschooled (colleges want an external benchmark)
• Your high school is not well-known to colleges (scores provide context)

Even if your target school is test-optional, a strong score can distinguish you from other applicants. It's extra evidence of your academic readiness.

When You Can Skip It

You can probably skip the test if:
• You have a strong GPA and rigorous course load
• You're applying to test-blind schools (UC system, Caltech, etc.)
• You're a poor test-taker and your practice scores are low
• The time and money required would cause significant stress

If you're not submitting scores, your other materials need to be strong. Your essays, activities, and recommendations matter more. Use the time you would have spent test-prepping to improve those areas.

The Score Choice Strategy

You can take the test multiple times and only submit your best scores. Most colleges allow Score Choice. Some require all scores (Georgetown, some public universities). Check each college's policy.

Plan to take the test twice. The first time, you learn what the test is like. The second time, you improve. Very few students improve on the third attempt. Don't take it more than three times.

Test-Prep Is Still Worth It (Even If You Don't Take the Test)

Studying for the SAT or ACT improves your reading, writing, and math skills. These skills help you in college. They help you on other standardized tests (like the GRE later). The studying is not wasted even if you don't submit scores.

If you decide to take the test, prepare systematically. Khan Academy is free and official. Practice tests are the best preparation. Take one, review mistakes, take another. Repeat.

The Scholarship Angle

Many scholarships require test scores. National Merit, state-funded scholarships, and university merit aid often use scores as criteria. If you need scholarships, you probably need to take the test.

Check the scholarship requirements for your target schools. Some colleges automatically consider you for merit aid based on your scores. Without scores, you might not be considered.

The Final Decision

Take a practice test under timed conditions. See where you score. If you're above the median for your target schools, take the real test. If you're below, decide whether you have time to improve.

If you're a junior, you have time. Take the test in the spring. See your score. Decide about retaking in the fall. If you're a senior, the clock is ticking. Register soon.

The SAT and ACT are not as important as they used to be. But for many students, they still matter. Take a practice test. Talk to your counselor. Make an informed decision. Don't just assume you can skip it.
